cuisine_type: Casual fine dining — Western technique with Ningbo / Chinese accents (French-leaning wine-bar menu)
address: Ground floor below Fabula, Weihai Lu, Jing'an (same building, Fabula upstairs)
head_chef: Zee Zheng (Ningbo native, former sous chef at Taian Table; also leads Fabula upstairs)
vibe_and_space: Intimate ~20-seat space, just six tables. Rather spartan but comfortable — focus stays on the food. French-chateau-heavy wine list. The format experiment is the building itself: Alea downstairs as the casual entry-point, Fabula upstairs as the fine-dining destination. Same chef, two price points, two formats.
one_paragraph_recommendation: Fabula's downstairs casual sibling under the same chef Zee Zheng — opened Nov 2024, predates Fabula's Michelin debut by a year. Western culinary technique applied to Ningbo flavors that Zheng grew up with: Sea Anemone Risotto, Hiramasa with persimmon, Lumina lamb loin, plus a stellar French-chateau wine list. À-la-carte at ¥168-358/dish vs Fabula's tasting menu (¥888-1288). The vertical Alea-downstairs / Fabula-upstairs format is itself the most interesting Shanghai chef-format experiment of 2024-2025 — same chef pedigree (ex-Taian Table) at two price points, two formats, sharing infrastructure. Recommend Alea when Fabula is booked out or when the occasion doesn't justify a tasting menu; the wine list alone is reason to come. Strongest signal: if Alea sustains at high quality, Zheng's vertical model becomes a templatable approach for future independent chef-driven openings — and the Alea-Fabula companion_pair is the worked example.
contradictions: Casual format obscures the chef-pedigree signal — diners may not connect Alea's ¥168 risotto to the same chef-hand behind Fabula's Michelin one-star tasting menu. Reservation pressure (and which space books out first) becomes a leading indicator of which format the market actually rewards in this chef-vertical model.
